A1 trial staged, A2 trial staged --> max cached actors = 2
A1 actor started --> unstaged --> max cached actors = 1
A1 actor stopped --> cached for reuse since A2 is staged
A2 actor started --> unstaged --> max cached actors = 0
A2 actor stopped --> not cached for reuse
A3 is pending and can be started with A1's cached actor
   - If we don't add A3 to staged trials, it'll encounter an error when we unstage the trial in `on_trial_reset` --> `actor_started` --> `unstage_trial_with_resources` (that's why we do `self._staged_trials.add(start_trial)`)
   - If we don't increase the max actors in the cache, it'll encounter an error when we unstage the trial and decrease the max cached actors to -1. That's why we add `self._actor_cache.increase_max(...)`.

I recommend using target validation to ensure that exactly one of those two fields is set:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/80bee0ad0977e83a2381530214fa3b362c805294/src/python/pants/engine/target.py#L590-L596

But hm, that is tricky that `source` has a default, which I think we really do want to keep as it's a great default the majority of the time. The Target API means that by the time you reach `Target.validate()`, you have no idea if the `Dockerfile` value is from the default vs. the user hardcoding it. So, two options:

1) Force the user to set `source=None`
2) Ignore `source` if `instructions` is set

Even though 1) is more boilerplate, I think that I prefer it a lot more. It's much more explicit, and it allows us to in the future allow both `source` and `instructions` at the same time. (Whereas it would be a breaking change to go from just ignoring `source` to now in the future using it.)

--

This conundrum does also give me some pause if Option A is better, as it avoids entirely any coupling, which is usually a smell with Target API modeling imo.

I'm still definitely open to Option C, although not 100% certain it's what I recommend

Generally speaking, using eval can be insecure and hard to maintain, and in almost every case there's a way to achieve the same functionality without eval. In this case, you can use some kind of simple filter language and filter the dataframe based on that. The user could provide a filter operation of the form `"<column> <operation> <value>"` (as a string), then we would convert that (without `eval`) into some kind of filter representation and then use it to filter the dataframe.

When you've got a successful result, you pass it back to parsl like this:
```
future.set_result(result)
```

If you want to indicate that the task has failed, instead set the future to an exception (the most python-like way):

```
future.set_exception(someexception)
```

or set the result using set_result to an instance of RemoteExceptionWrapper. This is a helper class that helps with some serialisation of exceptions from remote workers - so you might find it useful. Look in `executors/high_through/process_worker_pool.py` for an example of creating a RemoteExceptionWrapper.

Either of those methods for setting a failure will result in the exception you pass in popping out into the driving workflow script when future.result() is called.

Updated example, in a venv with Certbot installed using my PR for Windows file permissions:
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import os
import subprocess
import tempfile

from certbot.compat import security

CONTENT = '''\
echo Hello World!
'''

with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as path:
    test1 = os.path.join(path, 'test1.bat')
    test2 = os.path.join(path, 'test2.txt')

    with open(test1, 'w') as file_h:
        file_h.write(CONTENT)

    with open(test2, 'w') as file_h:
        file_h.write(CONTENT)

    print('Executable mode for test1 is: {0}'.format(os.access(test1, os.X_OK)))
    print('Executable mode for test2 is: {0}'.format(os.access(test2, os.X_OK)))

    try:
        subprocess.check_call([test1])
        print('test1 is effectively executable!')
    except OSError:
        print('test1 not so executable finally...')

    try:
        subprocess.check_call([test2])
        print('test2 is effectively executable!')
    except OSError:
        print('test2 not so executable finally...')

    security.apply_mode(test1, 0o600)

    print('Executable mode for test1 is: {0}'.format(os.access(test1, os.X_OK)))
    print('Executable mode with security module for test1 is: {0}'.format(
        any([security.check_mode(test1, 0o700), security.check_mode(test1, 0o400), security.check_mode(test1, 0o000)])))

    try:
        subprocess.check_call([test1])
        print('test1 still executable without executable bit!')
    except OSError:
        print('test1 is not not executable...')
```

You are not looking to the right problem here. It is true that by default, `open` will convert from and to a file any platform specific end of lines characters. So you will, from the python code, always handle with `\n`. But here we need to extract, as a list, every line. So `newlines` is involved, and it is its behavior that is problematic here: it will retrieve each line, with a end of line character at the end. It may or may not be platform specific depending on how `open` is invoked, but it will keep being a problem because we need to remove that end of line character.

So we need to remove it, and `strip()` will do that, for any end of line characters (here always `\n` because we are in test/universal newline mode).

As an alternative of that, I could use the shorter `lines = file_h.read().splitlines()`, that will do the two jobs (iterate and strip) in one action.

[3]: https://github.com/googleapis/google-cloud-python/blob/8517524cb08b85e4c97a5cfb6963b8d65cfa73f2/datastore/google/cloud/datastore/helpers.py#L336

One thing that is missing from this PR (and which is important) is to call this validation in CI. You want that to ensure people don't accidentally commit illegal files which might be hard to spot in a review (e.g. `4711.docs.rst`, `4711.fix.rst`, `4711.feat.rst` all *look*  ok to my tidy reviewer eyes but they are all wrong).   

Looking into the `tox.ini` shows that there's currently a `doctest` job.

https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/9ec8556ce48d52c858e09e2e2acd42bd71854cfb/tox.ini#L40

Maybe follow what we did with Trinity and turn that into a full `docs` job so that it combines all of this:

1. Build the docs 
2. Validate the doctest snippets
3. Validate the newsfragment files
4. Validate the release notes

There's one extra thing to point out here: `towncrier --draft` is to catch errors in the release notes but is has proven to be a weak solution so far. It doesn't catch actual errors in the RST. So what might happen is that someone adds a newsfragment file and missing a closing backtick or something like that.

We haven't got around fixing that yet but I believe the best case scenario would be to make the doc job be run like this:

1. Validate the doctest snippets
2. Run towncrier using a pseudo version (no `--draft`, right the actual files to disk)
3. Now build the docs
4. Validate the doctest snippets

Using that order, it would be guaranteed that any formatting errors in the release notes are caught by building the docs **after** the release notes where written to disk.

The one downside (which is discussed [here](https://github.com/ethereum/lahja/pull/153#issuecomment-521342852)) is that this command would leave the repository in a dirty state (modified release notes, deleted newsfragments). It's not a big deal in the CI run as nobody cares about the state of the files on a short-lived CI server disk but if someone would run the command locally that might be unexpected. 

To deal with that I'd recommend to do the following:

1. Do not allow the command to run when the repository is in a dirty state
2. Run a `git reset --hard` at the very end to not leave the repository in a dirty state

I'm not saying this PR needs to go that extra mile to do this form of validation, just pointing out what I believe is the best case scenario.

I do think so that the PR needs to ensure the basic validation is triggered in CI.

The code does not seem to handle the `E` flag case. As I mentioned from https://github.com/openthread/openthread/pull/5481#discussion_r493221529, if we see the `E` flag set we need to not only skip over the Type ID byte but also the byte that follows it.

Thread spec defines the behavior of "Type ID Flags" with `E` flag:
```1: An additional second flag byte after the current 1-byte flags```

Even though the use of `E` flag is reserved for future, we should parse such a case correctly and skip over two bytes (otherwise we can incorrectly handle the second byte as a new type id flag).

In C++ land you should be thinking "can this code crash?" all the time, regardless of using array indices or not. As evidenced by the last few commits in this PR. Modern C++ is a thin veil over the same crashy stuff. If someone changes the `num_returned` logic in your version, the code won't crash, but it'll silently do the wrong thing, so you need to be equally vigilant. In any case, I don't care enough about this case to force it, but watch out for buying more modernness than "Modern C++" can actually offer.

You can directly use the regex from your host pattern (instead of having a complex regex here, but actually parsing it later our of the host header).
If the region is not optional, you can directly assume the first part before the `.` is a region:
```
host="<region>.<api_id>.lambda-url.<regex('.*'):server>",
```
If it is optional, you can add two routes:
```
    ROUTER.add(
        "/<path:path>",
        host="<region>.<api_id>.lambda-url.<regex('.*'):server>",
        endpoint=handle_lambda_url_invocation,
    )
    ROUTER.add(
        "/<path:path>",
        host="<api_id>.lambda-url.<regex('.*'):server>",
        endpoint=handle_lambda_url_invocation
    )
```

This would allow you to:
- Get rid of the region parsing in the `request_context.py`
- Directly use the region and the API ID in the function signature of the endpoint function:
  ```
  def handle_lambda_url_invocation(request: Request, api_id: str, region: Optional[str] = None) ...
  ```
  Instead of:
  ```
  def handle_lambda_url_invocation(request: Request, **url_params: Dict[str, Any]) ...
  ```

Hi @yangmqglobe, thanks so much for following up here, many apologies for slow response. 

This is tricky, I will try to discuss this first:

> There is another question related to these code, too. Here, I filter out the lower level bins which might contain records that start before target region but overlap our target region.

From [Li (2011)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3042176/): "In the linear index, we keep for each tiling 16 kb window the virtual file offset of the leftmost record (i.e. having the smallest start coordinate) that overlaps the window." 

E.g., what if you have a VCF with a large duplication that starts at position 20 kb and ends at 40 kb, and all other records are SNPs. And then you query for variants in the region 35-50 kb. You take the query start (35 kb), floor divide by 16 kb, to get the corresponding window position within linear index, in this case 2, i.e., the third window, which spans 32-48 kb. You then access the third element within the linear index, which gives you the virtual file offset for the first variant overlapping the window 32-48 kb. This will be the virtual file offset for the record containing the large deletion starting at position 20 kb, because it is the first record overlapping the 32-48 kb window. 

In other words, I expect the linear index may give you variants that start before the query region. So filtering out results from the binning index won't stop this from happening. 

But as you also point out, the underlying VCF parsing code in scikit-allel will filter these out when it starts reading, so I don't think we have to worry about this. The code that handles this is [here](https://github.com/cggh/scikit-allel/blob/655254106c09a23ba9c6dc40c83b4a443a66bcfc/allel/opt/io_vcf_read.pyx#L1045). (As an aside, we might want to consider making this behaviour optional, so a user can retrieve all variants overlapping the query region like tabix does, not just those which strictly start within the query region, but that's a separate issue that could be address later.)

OK, I think I finally understood this. I got confused by "only on the first submodule level". 

I think what you're saying is: `west --rebase` can be repo-specific while `submodule --rebase --recursive` always performs a rebase, it's never repo-specific. That's one unavoidable inconsistency between `west` and `submodule`.

Considering `submodule --recursive` always performs the same action when traversing down, I think we all agree that all `submodule` invocations from west should all be given the same parameters, not depending on decisions `west` made in west repos. This keeps all submodules behaving the same no matter what.

However I think I disagree on never passing `--rebase` to `submodule`. I think it's more intuitive (and IMHO safer) to always pass `--rebase` through `west` and to `submodule` when and only when the user ask `west` to `--rebase`[*] 

Either way, the existence of some unavoidable inconsistency probably means  a "don't touch submodule" flag is needed sooner than later.

[*] I forgot why `west update --rebase` does not rebase detached HEADs. Feature or limitation?

One package per repository is more of a convention that CumulusCI assumes in a lot of places -- it's possible to imagine storing multiple packages in the same repository and setting up custom CumulusCI flows to work with them, but you would be "working against the grain" and not able to take advantage of the default flows that CumulusCI provides. So the main point is that you'll have a better experience with CumulusCI if you accept its premise that each package should go in its own repository.

Nice catch @wholmgren , I get about a 70x speed improvement (from 43.5 ms to 605us) to  by using numpy `datetime64` and `timedelta64[ns]`. I wonder where else we could use these? There is an oddity that `hours` is a `Float64Index` from pandas, not just floats, which bothers me, but ok for now.

See this script that shows improvements with numpy over looping.

```python
In [1]: import pandas as pd

In [2]: import datetime as dt

In [3]: import numpy as np

In [4]: d = pd.DatetimeIndex(start='2013-01-01 00:00:00', end='2013-12-31 23:59:59', freq='H', tz='Etc/GMT+8')

In [5]: h = np.loadtxt('../sunrise_sunset_transit.txt', dtype=np.dtype([('sunrise', float), ('sunset', float), ('transit', float)]))

In [6]: sr = pd.Float64Index(h['sunrise'])

In [7]: %paste
def _times(times, hours):
    return np.array([(dt.timedelta(hours=h) + t.tz.localize(
        dt.datetime(t.year, t.month, t.day)
    )) for h, t in zip(hours, times)])

## -- End pasted text --

In [8]: %timeit _times(d, sr)
43.5 ms ± 354 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each)

In [9]: del _times

In [10]: %paste
def _times(times, hours):
    """helper converts hours from an array of floats to localized times"""
    tz_info = times.tz
    return pd.DatetimeIndex(
        times.tz_localize(None).values.astype('datetime64[D]')
        + (hours.values * 3600. * 1.e9).astype('timedelta64[ns]'), tz=tz_info)

## -- End pasted text --

In [11]: %timeit _times(d, sr)
605 µs ± 5.07 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)
```

I agree in principle but I think it's worth clarifying on your response with respect to pickling objects through pipes.To me it would make sense to make it an option that defaults to pass the same error through.

More specficailly, only a short reference to `pytorch` is pickled and transferred through the pipe not the entire library as bytes. This reference is very small and is jsut a string representation of the module name to import. There is also the case that for a user to define the function, they need to import `pytorch` in the master process anywho:

```python
import pytorch

def f():
    # do pytorch things
    # raise PytorchError

limited_func = limit(f)
result = limited_func()  # Pytorch already exists in the master process.
```

The exception to this is of course imports that are scoped
```python
def f():
    import pytorch
    # do pytorch things
    # raise PytorchError

limited_func = limit(f)
limited_func()  # Pytorch now imported into the main process
```

However, if you're working with something like `pytorch`, you likely have it in the master process somewhere. The first use case seems like the most common and hence why I would make it an option that defaults to raise the same error class as the one in the subprocess

Yes, you need to use `yarn run`. Just so we are on the same page, let me be explicit.

`yarn` is synonymous as `npm install` which installs all dependencies according to yarn-lock.json (npm uses some other name for shrinkwrap file). After I updated package.json and yarn-lock.json, you _must use_  `yarn` to update your local dependencies. Do note that this operation is only useful for VS Code and other tools and _is not_ used by Bazel. Bazel manages its own `node_modules` folder inside bazel-* directory.

You may have read somewhere that installing node packages globally is warn against. I personally do not make use of it because I do not make sure to keep them up-to-date and they can pose security threats. 

When installing packages, if a package.json contains `bin` property, it will symlink the entry point as specified in the value of `bin` to `./node_modules/.bin/[package_foo_bin]`.  `yarn run ibazel`, `npm run ibazel`, or `npx ibazel`, modulo some gotcha, is synonymous as `./node_modules/.bin/ibazel run tensorboard ...`. Like you indicated elsewhere, if you really really want `ibazel` binary, you may use `npm i -g @bazel/ibazel` but I do not want to recommend that to everyone for the reasons I mentioned above.

You are basically duplicating the test code here, as your comment also hints at a little. In general it's advisable to limit the number of asserts and different logic/configurations in any given test. This limits the opportunities for a test to break, which eases diagnosis _if_ the test fails.

Two approaches to improve the situation are:
* To split this into two tests with different names to indicate the two different "purposes" for the tests. This can duplicate test code of course, which is typically not very desirable but can be worth it if the differences between the test implementations are larger.
* Another way, especially if the code of the two different alternatives is nearly identical, is to stack another `parametrize` along the different option set you choose (see the end of [this doc section](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/parametrize.html#pytest-mark-parametrize-parametrizing-test-functions)), which can be an elegant way to generate lots of combinations.

In this case, I think this should do the trick (decorating the test function, not the class, so only this test is affected)
```
@pytest.mark.parametrize("default_value", [True, False])
class TestBooleanInput:
    @pytest.mark.parametrize("value_remains_default", [True, False])
    def test_init_from_param(self, qtbot, default_value, value_remains_default):
``` 
then in the test do something like
```
if value_remains_default:
    ref = bool_param.default
else:
    ref = not bool_param.default
```
This should generate 4 tests with the approprite combinations without any duplicated test code. :-)

Hmm, let me try to explain my intent for how this class works. As a Subscriber, this class receives from its Publisher a Subscription, zero or more values, then (maybe) a Completion, in that order. Each value corresponds to one value that this class’s user demands via `Request`.

At present, all of this is synchronous, but the class is written to guard against a future case when all of this is asynchronous. In that scenario, the user can call `Request` (and generate a future) _before_ the class has even received a Subscription from its Subscriber. That’s the situation the code here (line 94) is handling. In this case, when we finally receive the Subscription, then we submit the demands to the Publisher to correspond to the requests our user has already made

```suggestion
1. The viewer’s web browser is redirected to Amazon Cognito custom UI page to sign up and authenticate.
2. After authentication, Cognito generates and cryptographically signs a JWT then responds with a redirect containing the JWT embedded in the URL.
3. The viewer’s web browser extracts JWT from the URL and makes a request to private content (private/* path), adding Authorization request header with JWT.
4. Amazon CloudFront routes the request to the nearest AWS edge location. The CloudFront distribution’s private behavior is configured to launch a `Lambda@Edge` function on ViewerRequest event.
5. `Lambda@Edge` decodes the JWT and checks if the user belongs to the correct Cognito User Pool. It also verifies the cryptographic signature using the public RSA key for Cognito User Pool. Crypto verification ensures that JWT was created by the trusted party.
6. After passing all of the verification steps, `Lambda@Edge` strips out the Authorization header and allows the request to pass through to designated origin for CloudFront. In this case, the origin is the private content Amazon S3 bucket.
7. After receiving response from the origin S3 bucket, CloudFront sends the response back to the browser. The browser displays the data from the returned response.
```

I answer here for `insert`, `append` and `extend`. These three functions have their type dictated by the superclass (`List`) which says that the actual type of `x` must be the same than the generic of the `List`. So to make x be of type `Union[str, None, List[Any]]`, I need to make `UnspacedList` heritate from `List[Union[str, None, List[Any]]]` instead of `List[Any]`.

This generates around 50 errors in the type checking basically because of the recursive type problem we already discussed in https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/9124#discussion_r778453975. Quite similarly, we have to stick with `List[Any]`, which consequently leads `x` to be `Any` in `insert`, `append`  and `extend`.

> having a different pattern for "core" (better name might be "default"?) fields than for extension fields might actually make things more challenging to document and explain.

Generally, users will always want to access `self.field_types`. This is what everything like `Target.get()` and `Target.has_sources()` uses. So, plugin fields do behave as first-class citizens.

We only differentiate between core fields vs. plugin fields at the constructor time so that:
1) We have better debugging. You can see in `repr()` which fields were added by you.
2) We can declare `core_fields` as a class var, whereas `plugin_types` must be an instance var because we have to inspect `UnionRule` at runtime. It's generally easier to work with class vars so we should use that where possible.

--

Why not unify plugin and core fields to always be set up via `UnionMembership`? Originally, I did take this approach. See the first few commits. But, it resulted in horrible verbosity and violated the principle of proximity. 

Instead of `core_fields: ClassVar = (HaskellGhcExtensions, HaskellSources)` in the class definition, we'd need `[UnionRule(HaskellTargetField, HaskellGhcExtensions), UnionRule(HaskellTargetField, HaskellSources)`. 

There's not a very compelling reason to have that verbosity + reduced proximity.

At least in normal JS development, when the repository has an existing
lockfile and `package.json` has not changed, running `yarn` is
guaranteed not to update the lockfile.\* This must be the case, because
the first thing that you do when cloning a JS repo is to run `yarn`, and
if that dirtied the lockfile then the kind of problem addressed by this
PR would be commonplace. Removing `node_modules` and running `yarn` to
re-populate it is also a common operation (at least among people like me
who routinely poke into `node_modules` for debugging), and that’s also
safe.

\* Unless you’re using a newer version of Yarn to run `yarn` than you
did when you originally generated the lockfile, in which case Yarn may
add integrity checks and stuff as migrations, but that’s kind of a
one-time thing and not a routine occurrence.

We definitely should make sure that this is hermetic, and definitely
shouldn’t update `yarn.lock` in unrelated commits. I think that the only
way that this could not do what we want is if the Bazel/`rules_nodejs`
integrations have some behavior that we don’t want, but Bazel is all
about hermeticity, so I wouldn’t expect that to be the case.

(@stephanwlee can correct me if any of this understanding is wrong.)

Gating on `package.json`/`yarn.lock` changes is certainly fine with me
if it’s necessary for some reason, and also okay with me if it’s not
strictly necessary but keeps us more comfortable, though it is a bit of
extra code that requires a bit of care to get the right diff range.

To clarify what the function actually does: It gives you the **bitwise** IEEE-754 presentation of a float.

Example:
```python
import struct


def __float_to_integer(value):
    return sum(b << 8 * i for i, b in enumerate(struct.pack("f", value)))


def __integer_to_binary(value, bit_length=32):
    return bin(value).replace("0b", "").rjust(bit_length, "0")


def encode(value: float):
    value = __float_to_integer(value)
    value = __integer_to_binary(value)
    return value


print(encode(1.0))
# output: 00111111100000000000000000000000
```
This should work, you can check it online, for example with: https://www.h-schmidt.net/FloatConverter/IEEE754.html.

the idea behind `Eager Worker Requesting` is to allow the request of a new worker even if the pipelines to existing ones are not full. This is useful when one (or more workers) have a load of many long tasks that can be better distributed by using more workers. For example, consider the scenario where we have max_tasks_in_flight=10, and the queue contains 5 long (say, 1s long) tasks. The owner will just send all the tasks to one worker (worker A), which will then have 5 tasks queued at the receiving end. In this situation, even if we don't have new tasks, it is useful to request a new worker B just so that B can steal some tasks from A and speed up the execution. This is what `Eager Worker Requesting` is designed for

Consider the following (actual) example: I want to run workers on the jagupard machines, which each have their own separate filesystem. I want the codalab workers to use the directory `/scr/scr-with-most-space` on each of the jagupard machines. So, I set that as the input work dir to the slurmworkermanager. However, I'm launching the slurmworkermanager from sc. It sees that I want to use `/scr/scr-with-most-space` as the work dir, so it tries to make `/scr/scr-with-most-space` on sc, not on the worker node. I don't have permissions to do this operation on sc, so it fails.

In general, I think the temporary directories we use to store sbatch scripts and other artifacts of the slurmworkermanager should be divorced from the directory that the worker, when it lands on a slurm node, runs its jobs in.

This looks really weird but is actually needed. Although the image is binary (`.convert("1")`), the data is stored as byte per pixel instead of one bit. Although `trans(img).dtype == torch.bool` in memory it is still stored as `255`. Naively doing `trans(img).to(torch.uint8)` would result in values of `0` and `255`.

Thus, we look at the values as if they are `uint8` (since they technically are) (`.view(torch.uint8)`), convert this to `bool` to write the proper values into the memory (`.bool()`) and finally convert it back to `uint8` (`.to(torch.uint8)`).

This never came up before, because `np.allclose` internally always converts both inputs to `np.float64`, whereas `torch.testing.assert_close` only converts to the result of [`torch.promote_type`](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.promote_types.html?highlight=promote_type), which gives `torch.uint8` for inputs of `torch.bool` and `torch.uint8`.

> The only nodes being "visited" in the proof (that is, the only path being traversed), is the one from the chain tip to the genesis block, which must include every block that was in the segmented proofs, correct ?

Yes.  But, the requirement that it "must include every block that was in the segmented proof" means that you will need to supply a sequence of sequences of hashes between two successive block hashes.  This is because you will almost certainly visit one or more intermediate blocks between blocks _i_ and _i+1_ while walking the skip-list, and you need to show that _those_ blocks exist as part of the fork as well.  That is, if you have the root hash for block 256 already, but don't have the root hash for 197, then the reason I can't just supply you the hashes for blocks 256, 224, 208, 200, 198, 197, and 0, but must instead supply all of the additional hashes I mentioned earlier, is because this isn't enough to convince you that block 208 was an ancestor of block 256.  You _do_ however know that 224 was an ancestor, because you know the hash of 256 and I can supply you the sequence of hashes that went into calculating it.  I can convince you that 224 is an ancestor of 256 this way, since I can't brute-force a hash-collision.  You can then ask me for the hashes that went into producing 224's hash, and from there, continue you walk back to 197.  This is why the Merkle proof for a MARF needs shunt proofs -- it's the standard way to prove that _A_ is the ancestor of _B_ in a Merkle skip-list for any nodes _A_ < _B_.

I understand your concern of wanting to be able to associate the template with a stack, so there are a few options I can think of:

```python
class CustomExecutor:
```

```
        _inf(project, 'Rebasing {name_and_path} to {manifest_rev_branch}')
```

I don't think you need to worry about this. For native speakers (in American English, at least...that website you linked to is from Australia 😁 ) bulleted lists don't really have formal rules.  Any mix of the following is equally valid:

* capitalizing vs. not capitalizing the first letter of each bullet point
* using a period at the end of each bullet point
* using semicolons at the end of the first `n-1` bullet points and a period at the end of the final one

So it's not that the current state on `master` is "right" and your proposal is "wrong". They're all equally valid ways to write a bulleted list, and I don't think it's worth spending time worrying about it.

Yes.

There's a further benefit too: as we prevent SimulationBuilder from directly accessing the internal implementation of TaxBenefitSystem, we make clear what the contract is for the dependency between SimulationBuilder and TaxBenefitSystem.

Eventually we'll notice that whenever we call `tax_benefit_system.something()` the something() has to do with the singular or plural of entities. The sum of these `something()`s defines an interface. This interface is all that we have to pass to SimulationBuilder rather than the entire TaxBenefitSystem. (The TaxBenefitSystem is needed by Simulation, but it could be passed to it by a different route.)

Suppose we call this interface EntityDictionary or something like that. Then to test SimulationBuilder we don't have to instantiate a full TaxBenefitSystem. We can instantiate the much smaller EntityDictionary. And we can instantiate it as a small fixture internal to the test, rather than having to take the entire country template. We get smaller, faster, more decoupled tests.

But the benefit also extends to the objects - we now have a way of assembling a TaxBenefitSystem in smaller steps: first we build an EntityDictionary, then we do the rest. Of course "the rest" can probably be split up as well. TaxBenefitSystem could be thought of as the combination of a VariableRepository, a ParameterRepository and possibly other things. 

All of these smaller objects are easier to unit test. We don't need to load the variables when we want to test parameters, and vice versa.

References: Martin Fowler's _Refactoring_ (2nd edition just came out), Bob Martin's _Clean Code_, Michael Feather's _Working Effectively With Legacy Code_, Sandi Metz's _Practical Object Oriented Design_.

This makes the API a bit confusing in my opinion. ``dns=True`` implies using libravatar, but only if it's installed, in which case ``dns`` is ignored. Given `spec = Provides(AClass, IFoo, IBar)`, the repr will be something like 
```
<zope.interface.Provides for instances of <class 'some.module.AClass'> providing (<InterfaceClass some.module.interfaces.IFoo>, <InterfaceClass some.module.interfaces.IBar>)>
```

Informative, but not terse.

Basically none of these objects have a `__str__` method.

`__repr__` is meant to "look like a valid Python expression that could be used to recreate an object with the same value (given an appropriate environment)."

In contrast `__str__`  computes "the 'informal' string representation of an object. This differs from `__repr__()` in that it does not have to be a valid Python expression: a more convenient or concise representation may be used instead. The return value must be a string object."

For `Provides`, it would be pretty easy to give a `repr` that actually can be evaluated (in many cases) outputting something like
```
Provides(AClass, IFoo, IBar)
```

Similarly, interfaces could have a `__str__` that just outputs `__module__.__name__`. 

That would transform reports from 
```
Object <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.ISet> has different legacy and C3 MROs:
  Legacy RO (len=7)                                  C3 RO (len=7; inconsistent=no)
  ====================================================================================================
    <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.ISet>            <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.ISet>
    <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.IKeySequence>    <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.IKeySequence>
  - <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.ISized>
    <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.ISetMutable>     <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.ISetMutable>
    <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.IKeyed>          <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.IKeyed>
    <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.ICollection>     <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.ICollection>
                                                     + <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.ISized>
    <InterfaceClass zope.interface.Interface>          <InterfaceClass zope.interface.Interface>
```

to
```
Object <InterfaceClass BTrees.Interfaces.ISet> has different legacy and C3 MROs:
  Legacy RO (len=7)                                  C3 RO (len=7; inconsistent=no)
  ==================================================================================
    BTrees.Interfaces.ISet            BTrees.Interfaces.ISet
    BTrees.Interfaces.IKeySequence    BTrees.Interfaces.IKeySequence
  - BTrees.Interfaces.ISized
    BTrees.Interfaces.ISetMutable     BTrees.Interfaces.ISetMutable
    BTrees.Interfaces.IKeyed          BTrees.Interfaces.IKeyed
    BTrees.Interfaces.ICollection     BTrees.Interfaces.ICollection
                                    + BTrees.Interfaces.ISized
    zope.interface.Interface          zope.interface.Interface
```

(With similar benefits when `Provides` is present.)

Since the changes in `Provides` here mean that there *might* have to be doctest updates, and since adding a `__str__` mostly shouldn't (but might!) cause doctest updates, this might be a good time to make such a change.

`simple` is added as a base alongside whatever class the user defined. If the user defined `__call__` then this will win, over the one in `simple`. We need the `__call__` attribute to point to the way of rendering the view, so the version in `simple` actually is perfect for what we need. In this case, we want to make the instance use the `simple.__call__` implementation rather than the one the class alone would pick.

The way I do this is by setting `__call__` on the instance to point at the right function. In Python3 we can just do `self.__call__ = simple.__call__` because in Python3 `type(simple.__call__)` is `<class 'function'>`. In Python2 it's `<type 'instancemethod'>`. This is to do with the whole new/old style class compatibility thing back in Python2.

This line uses the fact that instancemethods and functions are descriptors to explicitly bind it to the instance when setting it on the instance dictionary. If we don't do this then `self` looks like just another parameter to Python2 as it's not gone through the normal method resolution protocol.

Two tricks here.

First, enumerate has a two argument form: `iterable`, `start_number`. For us, `self.segments` is the iterable, since the `SegmentList` type can be iterated. We want to start counting from the media sequence number, so we use it as the second argument.

Second, the `self.media_sequence or 0` trick. I want to handle the case where `media_sequence` is `None` and treat that ias `0`, as per the HLS spec. The `or` operator takes the left argument if it's truthy (`None` isn't) and the right operator if it's not. 

```python
None or 0  # Picks 0
0 or 0  # Picks the second 0
1 or 0  # Picks 1
2 or 0  # Picks 2
# etc
```

I have no authoritative source, only some conventions that seem to point
in this direction. For instance, Werkzeug’s [`request.script_root`][1]
and [`request.path`][2] both normalize to be of `/leading-slash` form,
so it’s clearer if the underlying environment is already in that form.
And `/leading-slash` paths compose: `"/a" + "/b" == "/a/b"`, whereas for
example `"/a/" + "/b/" == "/a//b/"` is not what was wanted.

I’ll grant that the particular case of `eid=""` is awkward because there
is an actual empty path component, so the first point about Werkzeug
doesn’t apply. For consistency, I still think that this behavior makes
sense, but I could be convinced that a different behavior is ketter.

[1]: https://werkzeug.palletsprojects.com/en/0.15.x/wrappers/#werkzeug.wrappers.BaseRequest.script_root
[2]: https://werkzeug.palletsprojects.com/en/0.15.x/wrappers/#werkzeug.wrappers.BaseRequest.path

So, there are multiple places of caching in the current clique implementation but let's ignore the one's that are for performance and look at the [`HeaderCache`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/master/eth/consensus/clique/header_cache.py) which I think is the problematic one.

To recall, in Clique you can not validate the seal of a header unless you have access to the previous header. Let's assume we validate a series of headers via `validate_chain(...)`. These headers may span over multiple VMs and result in a `validate_seal(...)` call on each of these VMs. By the time that we validate the seal of the second header (or third, fourth, `n`s) the previous header won't be in the database yet (after all we are just in the middle of validating a whole series so non of it will get to the db until we've validated the entire series). So in order to look it up, we turn to our `HeaderCache` which we flush out as soon as we know that the headers are in the db (we actually keep the last `1000` in memory to account for temporary forks).

https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/843ab2969e2c47678185c940ea3a58d7693d453d/eth/consensus/clique/snapshot_manager.py#L72-L80

The other way to fix that that I see is to make `validate_chain` clique aware because that method has access to the entire series but then this would make the consensus engine operate on the chain level again.

`passthrough=True` gives the stdlib defaults. `passthrough=False` is equivalent to `input=b""` if no `input` is specified, plus `capture_output=True`. I explained above in this thread why I think it's error-prone to have different isolation defaults for input vs output.

My preference would be to have one flag that controls "are the standard streams for this process basically isolated or are they basically inherited from the parent Trio process", and then people can modify the treatment of individual streams with the stdin/out/err options if they don't want all three streams treated the same way. If the standard streams are isolated and you didn't provide any input, you probably want the subprocess's input to come from /dev/null. It seems confusing to me to distinguish `input=None` from `input=b""` for that purpose.

I think the core of the issue here is that `Visualize` is just `object`-type (which Marvin rightly points out no longer needs to be explicit), but then its public methods assume that the `self` argument should be of type `Atoms`. The original formulation of this PR (which I fussed at...) really instantiated the `Visualization` class and passed it an `Atoms` instance as data.

This smells like a messed up inheritance tree to me. The closest thing I could see would be having something like `_AtomsBase(ASEAtoms)`, `_AtomsVisualization(AtomsBase)`, `_AtomsAnalysis(AtomsBase)`, etc., then finally making our public-facing class incorporate all the functionality using multiple inheritance: `Atoms(_AtomsVisualization, _AtomsAnalysis)`. 

However, if we chose to go this multiple inheritance route I think it's something that could be built *on top of* this PR, rather than stopping this PR to do it that way.

My thinking here is very early in Certbot's execution for any subcommand, a config object is created through a combination of CLI params, global config file, and CLI defaults. this is heavily due to our use of reflection.  We mark it as non-nullable because it won't ever actually be null.  Specifically, the constructor will set all these `[Output(...)]` props to have non-null values.  

However, since that's happens using reflection, the compiler doesn't know about it and complains that no value is ever assigned to the prop. To shut it up, we both assign a value (`null`), and also include `!` to suppress the warning that we're assigning a null to something non-nullable.

Here, a major risk is that Pants auto-setting `--settings-path` means that there won't be a way to un-set that. The above advice to set `[isort].args` for more complex cases is bogus - that will not stop Pants from still setting the `argv` to use `--settings-path`. In my mental model with a setup like `/foo`, `/foo bar`, `/foo bar baz`

Old order:

- `/foo` gets copied. 
- Parent for `bar` is set to `/foo`. 
- `bar` is copied (parent properly assigned)
- Bug: Children are copied (but parent is not properly assigned)

New order:

- `/foo` gets copied
- `bar` parent is the *old* `/foo`
- `bar` is copied
- children are copied and their parent is set to the copy

The issue here is that `parametrize_with_cases` already has a `prefix` argument, and this is **always** used. Besides, it is used before the filters are called. Finally it does not take into account overridden `id`s.

So I suggest that we modify this so that it acts on the case id - that will complement nicely the `prefix` argument in order to work on the case `id`, not on the case function name.

```suggestion
def has_id_prefix(prefix):
    """Select cases that have a case id prefix `prefix`. Note that this is not the prefix of the case function, just the id (once the case prefix has been removed)"""
    return CaseFilter(lambda case: CaseInfo.get_from(case).id.startswith(prefix))
```

Note that tests should be updated accordingly, and the "suffix" filter too.

IIUC, the use case is to transform multiple images with the same randomly sampled parameters, correct? If yes, this already works:

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/95d418970e6dbf2e4d928a204c4e620da7bccdc0/torchvision/prototype/transforms/_transform.py#L22-L23

We sample the parameters once for the complete input and afterwards apply the transformation with these sampled parameters to all inputs individually. For example, let's say you want to randomly rotate two images the same way:

```py
transforms = RandomRotate(...)

image1, image2 = transform(image1, image2)
```

This of course also means we need no extra handling for image pair datasets:

```py
transform = RandomRotate(...)
dataset = datasets.load("sintel").map(transform)

for sample in dataset:
    ...
```

So my reasoning for doing this (and not just making my own widget that inherits from foreignkeywidget) is that the way widgets are structured you have to duplicate almost the entire logic and that's a pretty horrible DRY violation -- I found that when I was writing my own subclassed widgets and fields that subclassing them meant usually rewriting the entire clean and render method (or save in the case of fields), which would then be out of date when the parents were updated.  

In Render specifically you have the whole attrs splitting logic and the try/except block that then has to be changed multiple places if Django changes.

The other major advantage to using ForeignKeyWidget is that if we were to add a  _meta flag to the resourceoptions, we could force an entire model to default to using natural keys without having to write another widget detection function. I think that'd make for quite a bit cleaner interface with less to maintain than another widget detection function, another widget, and so on.  

I do not think this could break any existing subclassed ForeignKeyWidgets unless someone explicity passed it a parameter it couldn't support, but might be missing something. 

I think the functionality of using django natural key functions is useful enough that I'm happy to rewrite it that way I just worry about how wet that widget will be. It wouldn't be able to use the inherited widget's functionality outside of the __init__ (whereas with this design it reuses most of the code).

I think we should spend a little more time thinking about how we expect users to interface with this class. I'm not talking about passing in all the configuration dicts, but more how a Trainer would be implemented in practice. I'm assuming we have a lot of default implementations in place, but I'd argue that we should still make it obvious how to extend a `Trainer` for a new, fancy framework.

In principle, all users have to do is to implement `as_trainable`, which seems easy enough at first, but introduced a million obstacles down the line. Users either need to know a) how to define a `tune.Trainable`, i.e they have to know that they need to implement a `step`, `save_checkpoint` and `from_checkpoint` method, or b) how to specify a function which can be wrapped as `FunctionRunner` with the right tooling. But this is all quite a lot to ask.

Can we e.g. instead have a method called `training_step`, which returns a function and we simply use `wrap_function` internally to make this a `FunctionRunner`? Would we lose a lot by restricting ourselves to functions (instead of full-blown Trainables)? I think a `Trainer` that has to simply define a `training_step` is a _lot_ more intuitive then what we're proposing currently.

```suggestion
    """Generate a learning-to-rank dataset - feature vectors grouped together with
    integer-valued graded relevance scores. Replace this with a sklearn.datasets function
    if ranking objective becomes supported in sklearn.datasets module.

    Parameters
    ----------
    n_samples : int, optional (default=100)
        Total number of documents (records) in the dataset.
    n_features : int, optional (default=20)
        Total number of features in the dataset.
    n_informative : int, optional (default=5)
        Number of features that are "informative" for ranking, as they are bias + beta * unif(min=y, max=y + 1),
        where bias and beta are standard normal variates. If this is greater than n_features, the dataset will have
        n_features features, all will be informative.
    group : array-like, optional (default=None)
        1-d array or list of group sizes. When `group` is specified, this overrides n_samples, random_gs, and
        avg_gs by simply creating groups with sizes group[0], ..., group[-1].
    gmax : int, optional (default=2)
        Maximum graded relevance value for creating relevance/target vector. If you set this to 2, for example, all
        documents in a group will have relevance scores of either 0, 1, or 2.
    random_gs : bool, optional (default=False)
        True will make group sizes ~ Poisson(avg_gs), False will make group sizes == avg_gs.
    avg_gs : int, optional (default=10)
        Average number of documents (records) in each group.

    Returns
    -------

        Input feature matrix for ranking objective.
    y : 1-d np.array of shape = [n_samples (or np.sum(group))]
        Integer-graded relevance scores.
    group_ids: 1-d np.array of shape = [n_samples (or np.sum(group))]
        Array of group ids, each value indicates to which group each record belongs.
    """
```

> Also I thought cast takes a type? i.e. cast(int, x)... or the no-op int(x).

`cast` is a noop (does nothing) while `int()` actually does something. cast() should be faster because it does literally nothing at runtime. Also `'int'` and `int` is effectively the same for mypy (in most cases), changing to `int` to avoid confusion.

> mypy casts are only used as hints for the type checker, and they don’t perform a runtime type check https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/casts.html

> Return an integer object constructed from a number or string x, or return 0 if no arguments are given. https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#int

```suggestion
* Network (the network in front of your system as well as the NICs [physical or virtual] themselves)
* Disk IO 
* file limits (nofile in Linux)
* RAM. Even if you have oomkiller turned off as suggested, the unavailability of RAM could cause issues.
* CPU
* Virtualization Contention (overcommitting the hypervisor)
* Any cgroup limitation (likely to be related to the above)
* ``kill -9`` of any postgres process (except postmaster!). This is a decent simulation of a segfault.

One thing that you should not do is run ``kill -9`` on a postmaster process. This is because doing so does not mimic any real life scenario. If you are concerned your infrastructure is insecure and an attacker could run ``kill -9``, no amount of HA process is going to fix that. The attacker will simply kill the process again, or cause chaos in another way.
```

```py
import torchvision.models as M
M.detection  # <- directly accessing the attribute
```

but in a lot of patterns this gets bypassed, typically when doing

```py
from torchvision.models.detection import ssd
```

or even just

```py
import torchvision.models.detection
```

Thanks for giving it a thought, @Eric-Arellano! Just to provide more context:

Given this declaration:

```
debian_package(
    name='sample-debian-package',
    description='Useful tools installed as Debian package.',
    packages=[':files'],
    sources=['sample-debian-package-debian-sources/**/*'],
) 
```

User can do `./pants package //:sample-debian-package` (using the `name` from the declaration) to build a Debian package. The standard way is that there is a single directory that will be packaged as a Debian package and it is expected that this directory contains `DEBIAN/control` file. That's the absolute minimum. 

The user may decide to put some extra helper files into it, if desired. Here there is `/etc` directory inside the directory to be packaged:

```
$ tree sample-debian-package      
sample-debian-package
├── DEBIAN
│   └── control
└── etc
    └── configs
        └── config.rc
```

Therefore, the whole directory `sources=['sample-debian-package-debian-sources/**/*']` must be available upon packaging. I am happy with how things work now in this draft PR, with the only exception that sources ideally should be limited to the single directory (with all its content). People who are familiar wouldn't be passing arbitrary files from various locations in the repository because they keep all their resources in a single directory, dedicated to a particular Debian package, but it would be nice to enforce the constraints anyway.

This function is only called internally by algebra operators/query compiler/IO functions, and the type hint is essentially always a scalar dtype like `np._bool`. Consequently, I there shouldn't be any code path where `dtypes` is neither a scalar type nor `pandas.Series`. This elif branch handles the scalar case, and the fall-through direct passing to the dataframe constructor handles the latter.

I think we ran into a similar issue with deserializing asset backfills in the past.

The solution we landed on was to serialize (a) the partitions def serializable unique ID and (b) class name for the partitions subset for each partitions subset. This enables:
- detecting when a partitions def has been removed.
- for time window partitions subsets, checking the serializable unique ID to see if the partitions def is still the same. If it is not, we can no longer deserialize.
- for other partitions subsets (static/multipartitioned), if the class name is equivalent, we can deserialize since it just stores a set of partition keys.

This is all logic that already exists in `partitions_def.can_deserialize_subset`. I suspect we'll likely want to implement something similar for these cases, though it's up to you on if you want to implement this now or in the future.

I'm curious the reasoning behind removing support for this.

I think it's fairly common in FastAPI world to want to allow extra fields to be passed into your API (e.g. so you can remove ignored fields from your model without it causing errors), and there have been many issues over the years of people wanting to use dataclasses instead of BaseModel in their fastapi endpoints. 

And today, you can allow extra for dataclasses in FastAPI:
```
import pydantic.dataclasses
from dataclasses import dataclass

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

app = FastAPI()


@pydantic.dataclasses.dataclass
class MyBody:
    x: int


@app.post("/items/", include_in_schema=False)
async def read_items(body: MyBody):
    return {"x": body.x}


client = TestClient(app)

response = client.post("/items/", json={'x': 1, "y": 2})
assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
assert response.json() == {"x": 1}, response.json()
```

I would be amenable to the idea of dropping support for this if it causes serious issues (or if the plan is to do something different for FastAPI validation, somehow). I think if we want to make this change though and won't support extra fields for FastAPI body arguments annotated with dataclasses, we should probably run that by Sebastian for feedback.

> But what about when there _is_ data in the buffer? What if there were an infinite amount of 'junk'?

@Tbruno25 

```py
def read(self, __size: int = ...) -> bytes: ...
def read_all(self):
    return self.read(self.in_waiting)
```
1. If there is data in the buffer, the `read` method only copies it out. Only when the data bytes in the buffer are smaller than the parameter `__size`, the `read` method will blocking until the data bytes in the buffer is `__size`, then copy it out.
2. Since the `in_waiting` parameter shows the current number of bytes in the buffer, blocking will not occur. (The time to copy data out is negligibly small)
3. According to my test, the `MAX` receive buffer size in win10 is `16384`, and ubuntu 20.04 is `4095`. I'm not sure whether it can be changed. But I think there will not be `infinite` junk.

Technically yes, but that is also true prior to this change - I can set the version (and extra_requirements) to whatever I want and generate a (now old-style) "tool lockfile" off that.

Right now our tool requirements tend to be overly constrained, e.g., [black](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/5f3f251a378f780daac07e0e5409c9a132571a89/src/python/pants/backend/python/lint/black/subsystem.py#L54), [pytest](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/d39ad4b252578e20bc0f9e154607be165afb4949/src/python/pants/backend/python/subsystems/pytest.py#L78), or, slightly more permissively, but not much, [isort](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/7251de9bb8aef692b4ffcfc62c36943983a51145/src/python/pants/backend/python/lint/isort/subsystem.py#L31). 

So if we use these as-is, users will have to tinker with `version` (and/or `extra_requirements`) to loosen them up as well as specify versions (or possibly tighter version ranges) for them in some requirements.txt.

We could audit all the tools and specify loose requirements directly on `default_requirements`, but perhaps in a followup? There are a lot of them. 

And this also raises the question of how would we do so? We can pick a minimum version that we know we're compatible with, but how would we pick an upper-bound? In some cases we can assume semver, perhaps, so we can bound at the next major version. But then that may be quite restrictive, because in practice it might be likely that Pants will work with the next major version, and we've forced users, again, to update `requirements` as well as a requirements.txt. And the whole point here was to avoid that sort of near-repetition. Granted they might not need to update `requirements` as often as the more specific version in requirements.txt, perhaps only when they want to bump the major version.

So I'm a little torn about what the right thing to do is.

As mentioned in comments & in the code, PYTHON_BASE_EXE / sys._base_executable / or even os.path.realpath(sys._base_executable) won't have psutil installed, by design (it's the global python interpreter, not the venv) & that's why we need 2. In this PR, only `pyrun` reinjects the `__PYVENV_LAUNCHER__` to get psutil importable when using PYTHON_BASE_EXE.
If we don't use the global python interpreter, we can't count processes (or we'd need a specific workaround) or rely on the pid of the launched interpreter to count connections. If we use the global interpreter, we can't import psutil.
My conclusion, getting 2 is the easiest, otherwise, we'd need 1 + an environment variable or rework the failing tests but I feel the 2 interpreters is easier. We can't get away from those issues just by modifying PYTHON_EXE.

Let me add a bit more info here to have a bit more context.

If we had a way to detect that a certain Tensor object is a "simple tensor" while JIT-scripting, then we would be able refactor the entire `F` API to keep it JIT-scriptable and maintain BC. This could require some nasty tricks like:
- Faking types
- Splitting the non-jit scriptable part of `F` high-level kernels in separate methods that are marked with @torch.jit.unused. 
- Extensive use of `torch.jit.is_scripting()` and `torch.jit.is_tracing()`

Another thing we could do if we want to avoid BC breakages is to provide a jit-scriptable `F` that has exactly the same signatures as the high-level kernels but only supports pure tensors.

To keep the above a possibility, we shouldn't modify prematurely the existing signatures to receive parameters that are not JIT-scriptable (like `Sequence`). We might end up doing it at the end, but let's hold off to that until we know for sure.

Suppose we have two Person nodes with names Albert and Betty such that there's an edge Albert-knows->Betty. 

Then we run a query

```
MATCH (p:Person {name:"Albert"})
OPTIONAL MATCH (p)-[r:KNOWS]->(p2:Person)
where p2.name="Charles"
return p,r,p2
```

Neo4j returns `Albert` for `p`, `null` for `r`, and null for `p2`.

In this case, we do have an "knows" edge from Albert to some other person but the filter fails because the other person isn't named Charles. However, if Albert has no outgoing "knows" edges, then the filter wouldn't apply at all, so effectively Neo4j pretends that the outgoing edge doesn't exist.

So I've been mulling this over for a while, and having looked at `http_source`, I can see how it works, and could apply here. I'm happy to implement it if the discussion lands on implementing it in this way.

I've re-written this comment a few times, to make sure that my understanding of how things work lines up with what's actually going onside Pants. Some of the objections I initially had turned out to not be entirely correct. 

I think there's some complication however, that justifies the conceptual separation of our `sources` target generators and a corresponding multiple source wrapper targets. Count me as a medium _no_ against adding the new value type.


Most `x_sources` file types (which produce `x_source` targets) are `TargetFilesGenerators`, which currently produce `GeneratedTargets` representing one target per file matched by the generator. Eventually, the `MultipleSourcesField` on the target is used in the `GeneratedTargets`-producing rule to resolve a `SourcesPaths`, which does not consider codegen.

It would be possible to amend that `-> GeneratedTargets` rule to act as a no-op if the `MultipleSourcesField`'s value were an `output_sources`. I presume it would be possible to implement a `GeneratedSources` rule for each of the `MultipleSourcesField`s that we end up wanting to support. Rules would need to be registered per `x_sources` target type, since codegen requires a single rule per `(InputSourceField, OutputSourceField)` pair. This can't be as neat a solution as the `TargetFilesGenerator` approach is for generating targets for actual files. 

The conceptual gap here is the `x_sources` Target would be a Target Generator when operating on source files, but a plain old `Target` that resolves to several (codegenned) source files when using an `output_sources` value. This makes it difficult to reason about the behaviour of this target type, and similarly difficult to document: This would significantly complicate the documentation for each of these `x_sources` targets. It would need to _explain_ that the target behaves significantly differently depending on whether you're using it for real files or to wrap generated files from elsewhere.

It also looks as though the behaviour of `moved_fields` and `copied_fields` would also be somewhat difficult to reason about -- they'd be neither moved, nor copied if the target is used directly? It seems like `parametrize` also behaves weirdly here.

So I think an implementation using the existing target generators with a new `output_sources` value type would (at the moment) comfortably land in the "difficult to explain" bucket. We are doing something significantly different to what the target types currently do.

On the other hand, the current implementation produces a bunch of target types (not ideal), but they're easier to reason about, and they're all grouped in help under `experimental_wrap_as`, which should make them easy-ish to spot in the help. 


**This is `experimental`, we can change the API later**

Currently all of the wrapper types will be marked `experimental`, which would allow us time to deal with some of the above items where possible. With that in mind, we could land this as currently proposed, and take the time to solve the above technical blockers.

This fixes a bug I noticed where `hiddenKeys` is being updated in a loop with the same values. It appears to be happening because of the `onStateChanged`, which pushes updates to the hidden and visible keys, which results in an update to context, which pushes a new state to the filter component, which triggers `onStateChanged`, etc.

I think there's always a bit of risk with setting the global hidden/visible key state, as any caller might set the hidden repo keys at any time and at any cadence, and there's nothing stopping the entire workspace context from updating at that cadence.

We can at least guard against this flavor of loop (where the same list is set in state repeatedly) by stringifying the array and using that for `useMemo` comparison. This way, if the list of hidden and visible keys is the same, we won't update the `visibleRepos` array at all, and the filter component won't push a new state value.

This is why we need cleaner naming conventions, imo:
* `save()`/`export_checkpoint()` -> write stuff to disk
* `get_state()` -> return a dict; nothing written to disk

multiple queries can be ok when you know how many of them you need. in this case, we can easily have hundreds or thousands of them. Think a particularly broken AOSP build where 1% of its 1M tests fail. Then you have 1000 queries.

In this case you can solve it with 2 queries: 1 for "which tests fail on the current build", and 1 for "get the history of this set of tests for the latest N builds", and then you can do the confidence calculation on the python side. The 2nd query is similar to the one you have inside the loop, but with `metadata__in=[t.metadata for t in failing]` instead of `metadata=t.metadata`. Make sure to augment the first query with `prefetch_related("metadata")` as well to avoid have the same 100 queries but against the metadata table.

```python
def compose(input: Tensor,
    size: Tensor) -> Tensor:
  new_height, new_width, = torch.unbind(size)
  height_scale = torch.div(new_height, CONSTANTS.c0)
  width_scale = torch.div(new_width, CONSTANTS.c0)
  data = torch.clone(input)
  data0 = torch.to(data, torch.device("cpu"), 6)
  old_x1, old_y1, old_x2, old_y2, = torch.unbind(torch.detach(data0), -1)
  a = torch.mul(old_x1, width_scale)
  b = torch.mul(old_y1, height_scale)
  c = torch.mul(old_x2, width_scale)
  d = torch.mul(old_y2, height_scale)
  data1 = torch.stack([a, b, c, d], -1)
  data2 = torch.to(data1, torch.device("cpu"), 6)
  x, y, x2, y2, = torch.unbind(torch.detach(data2), -1)
  c0 = torch.sub(x2, x)
  d0 = torch.sub(y2, y)
  data3 = torch.stack([x, y, c0, d0], -1)
  data4 = torch.to(data3, torch.device("cpu"), 6)
  x0, b0, w, d1, = torch.unbind(torch.detach(data4), -1)
  a0 = torch.rsub(torch.add(x0, w), 5)
  data5 = torch.stack([a0, b0, w, d1], -1)
  data6 = torch.to(data5, torch.device("cpu"), 6)
  x1, y1, w0, h, = torch.unbind(torch.detach(data6), -1)
  c1 = torch.add(x1, w0)
  d2 = torch.add(y1, h)
  data7 = torch.stack([x1, y1, c1, d2], -1)
  data8 = torch.to(data7, torch.device("cpu"), 6)
  return torch.detach(data8)
```

```python
new_height, new_width, = torch.unbind(size)
height_scale = torch.div(new_height, CONSTANTS.c0)
width_scale = torch.div(new_width, CONSTANTS.c0)
```

```python
old_height, old_width = input.image_size
new_height, new_width = size
```

A note on bytecode patching. We did it at some point in HDiv before moving to AST patching (which for Chapter 0 would not be needed) and getting it right for all cases of function substitution/alteration is almost impossible at the bytecode level, the problem being is that you sometimes have to "backtrack" for example for things like fstrings, function calls in arguments (that could be recursive), classes implementing __call__, and specially (cold sweat) trying to replace things like `+` or `*` or other opcodes for strings for functions. Sometimes that backtracking is not perfect (you can easily interpret it forward but not always so easily backwards unless you keep a full reversible state of the interpreter on memory). For future Python versions there is people working on sort of bytecode plugins (sort of like Rust interpreter plugins) that could serve us at some point, but right now... it gives me PTSD.

For this specific use case of detecting weak hashes however it could work since it doesn't involve most things that we do for IAST, just replacing some functions but I see wrapt as an easier solution. `inputDependencies` is documented in the .proto:

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/3dde9d02bf0b3fd84ab9630ef33cb7aecc99cf67/sdk/proto/provider.proto#L267

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/3dde9d02bf0b3fd84ab9630ef33cb7aecc99cf67/sdk/proto/provider.proto#L251-L254

If the property was assigned an output of another resource, or the result of an `apply` from one or more outputs, then the property will have an associated list of resource URNs that the property depends on. If there aren't any dependencies associated with the property, then we don't need to project it as an `Output` because we don't have any dependencies to track. Unless it's a secret: secrets in the type system are typed as `Output<T>` (we did this rather than having a separate `Secret<T>` type to keep the programming model simpler), so in that case, we need to wrap the value as `Output<T>`.

We don't have a specific flag that says "Property 'foo' was set a plain value", so instead, we just see whether the raw gRPC value can remain a plain value, otherwise we wrap it as an output. We're sort of relying on the type system of the generated SDK to indicate that the type of the value should be `T` and not `Input<T>`, but in some languages that don't have a real type system (e.g. JavaScript/Python), someone could force passing an `Output`, so right now we're sort of relying on type checking to prevent that. We should probably add some runtime type checking for JS/Python for plain values, to further help prevent that from happening.

> Would this code confuse `T` and `Input<T>` or it does not matter?

`Input<T>` can be `T` or `Output<T>`:

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/3dde9d02bf0b3fd84ab9630ef33cb7aecc99cf67/sdk/nodejs/output.ts#L727

If an input property is typed as accepting an `Input<T>`, it can be given a `T`, and it'll _just work_.

@NikolajBjorner I left this constructor in, but constructors like this are not needed. C++11 introduces initializer lists which have a few benefits. For example, `A a{1, 2}` would correctly initialize `struct A { int a; int b };` The compiler will implicitly generate a constructor with `noexcept` and everything it can. Having explicit constructors for pure data copying like this is actually against cpp guidelines. That said if we are upping to C++11 just now, people using the older `()` syntax for constructors might run into a harder time; that's why I left it in. If you have any objections to this though since it is bad practice let me know.
